mnemonics.title: IF-ELSE Order Reminder
mnemonics.content: I F I R S T — IF comes first, then ELIFs, then ELSE last. Remember: IF = Initial check, ELIF = Extra checks, ELSE = Everything else (the fallback)

mnemonics.title: COLON AND INDENT Magic
mnemonics.content: Every IF/ELIF/ELSE line needs a COLON (:) at the end, then INDENT the block below. No colon = no code block, no indent = not in block!

mnemonics.title: MATCH-CASE Pattern
mnemonics.content: MATCH the variable, CASE the possible values. Like a switch but smarter — Python finds the first matching case and runs it. Wildcard using case _: acts as the else!

mnemonics.title: Truthy-Falsy Quick Check
mnemonics.content: If it EXISTS and is NOT empty → TRUTHY. If it's ZERO, EMPTY, or NONE → FALSY. Ask: 'Does it have value?'

acronyms.usage: These 6 values evaluate to False in boolean context. Use for checking: if not list: (empty list is falsy), if not name: (empty string is falsy)
All conditions must be satisfied. Example: if age >= 18 AND has_license: → Both must be true to drive
At least one condition must be true. Example: if student OR senior: → Get discount if either applies
Converts True to False and vice versa. Example: if not logged_in: → Check if user is NOT logged in

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a fork in the road. The IF condition is a question at a junction. TRUE takes the LEFT path (do this), FALSE takes the RIGHT path (do that). Draw a diamond shape for the decision point.
Think of a staircase or ladder. You start at the bottom (IF). Each ELIF is another step up — you climb only if you didn't pass the previous step. ELSE is the final landing at the top.

Picture a vending machine or a locker bank. You insert a value (the subject), and it automatically goes to the matching compartment. The default case (case _) is the 'other' bin for anything that doesn't match.
Visualize a balance scale. == checks if both sides balance (equal). > checks if left pan is heavier. < checks if right pan is heavier. >= means left is heavier OR balanced.

analogies.analogy: A restaurant bouncer: IF (age >= 18) → Enter club; ELSE → Go home. Simple yes/no decision at the door.
Traffic light sequence: IF (green) → Go; ELIF (yellow) → Slow down; ELIF (red) → Stop; ELSE → Wait (default safety). Each condition checked in order until one matches.
Hotel room key card — MATCH (room number), CASE (floor). Each room number maps to a specific floor. If room 101 → Floor 1, room 202 → Floor 2, case _ → Reception (default).
AND = Both hands needed to clap (both must happen). OR = Either umbrella OR raincoat (any one works). NOT = Looking in a mirror (reverses the result).
Airport security check: First IF (boarding pass valid?), then inside that, another IF (luggage checked?), then another IF (items allowed?). Like Russian dolls — one inside another.
